---
title: "Foundations"
subtitle: "Embrace the good, engineer around the bad"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, theme.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    incremental: false
    transition: fade
    code-line-numbers: false
---

# LLM capabilities are _jagged_ {.center}

::: notes
The single most important mental model for this whole day. "Jagged" comes from
Ethan Mollick's research — the idea that LLM capability isn't a smooth gradient
from easy to hard. Some tasks that *feel* trivial to humans are brutally hard
for LLMs, and vice versa.
:::

## The strawberry problem {.center}

> How many r's are in "strawberry"?

::: {.fragment}

Most LLMs confidently answer **2**.

The correct answer is **3**.

:::

::: {.fragment}
An "easy" task for a 5-year-old. A hard task for a trillion-parameter model.
:::

::: notes
Live demo opportunity: actually ask an LLM this in real time if you want.
Recent models have gotten better at this specific example (it's become a
benchmark), but the underlying point stands: tokenization means LLMs don't
"see" individual characters the way we do. This is a symptom, not a fluke.
:::

## But also... {.center}

::: {.placeholder style="min-height: 350px;"}
SCREENSHOT: an impressive Claude Code or Copilot session —<br>
a complex coding task completed in seconds<br>
(e.g., "build me a Shiny app that does X" → working app)
:::

A "hard" task for most humans. Trivial for an LLM.

::: notes
The juxtaposition is the point. The same system that can't count letters can
write a working web app from a paragraph of prose. The audience should feel
this tension viscerally — it's what motivates everything we build today.
:::

## The jagged frontier {.center}

::: {.placeholder style="min-height: 400px;"}
IMAGE: "Jagged frontier" illustration —<br>
a line graph with "task difficulty (human perception)" on x-axis<br>
and "LLM performance" on y-axis, showing a jagged, non-monotonic curve<br>
(Source: Ethan Mollick / "Co-Intelligence", or recreate as a simple sketch)
:::

::: notes
Credit Ethan Mollick here. The frontier is jagged — there are pockets of
superhuman performance next to pockets of embarrassing failure. Our job as
engineers is to put LLMs to work in the high-performance pockets and build
guardrails around the low ones.
:::

## The key insight {.center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

### LLMs are great at

- Language → code
- Summarization
- Pattern matching
- "Fuzzy" translation

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

### LLMs are bad at

- Precise counting / math
- Faithful recall of facts
- Consistent long-range state
- Knowing what they don't know

:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Our strategy:** focus LLMs on what they're good at, and **engineer around** the rest.
:::

::: notes
This two-column framing is the actionable takeaway from the jagged frontier.
The left column is where we'll aim chatlas, shinychat, and querychat — we're
asking LLMs to *translate user intent into code*, not to *be* the database or
*be* the calculator. The right column is what we'll build harnesses around.
:::

## What is an agent? {.center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5.5
flowchart TD
    U["👤 User"] --> |prompt| L["🧠 LLM"]
    L --> |tool call| T["🔧 Tools"]
    T --> |result| L
    L --> |response| U
    style L fill:#447099,stroke:#33567a,color:#fff
    style T fill:#ee6331,stroke:#c84e1f,color:#fff
    style U fill:#0e3b5c,stroke:#082638,color:#fff
```

:::
::: {.column width="45%"}

An **agent** is an LLM, in a **harness**, that calls **tools** in a loop — deciding each next step from the last result.

- **Read** tools observe the world
- **Write** tools change the world

:::
:::

::: notes
Definition from Joe Cheng's "What is an agent?" post. Keep the diagram on
screen and walk through it: the user prompts, the LLM decides what tool to
call, the tool returns a result, the LLM decides what to do next. The "loop"
part is what separates an agent from a single LLM call.

Read vs. write tools: reading a file, searching the web, querying a database
= read tools. Writing a file, sending an email, making an API call = write
tools. The risk profile is very different.
:::

## Agent harnesses are getting good {.center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.placeholder style="min-height: 300px;"}
SCREENSHOT: Claude Code terminal session<br>
(something relatable — fixing a bug, writing tests)
:::
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.placeholder style="min-height: 300px;"}
SCREENSHOT: OpenAI Codex or similar<br>
(another agent IDE in action)
:::
:::
:::

This has **radically changed** how I work in the last 6 months.

::: notes
Be personal here. Talk about your actual experience — what changed in your
workflow, what surprised you, what you'd never go back from. The audience is
data scientists who probably use ChatGPT but haven't let an agent loose on
their codebase yet. Your honest enthusiasm (and healthy caution) is the
credibility play.
:::

## YOLO mode {.center}

::: {style="font-size: 1.8em; font-weight: 700; margin-bottom: 0.5em;"}
Let the agent run unsupervised.
:::

Terrifying. But _so_ useful.

::: {.fragment}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### ✅ Viable when...
- **Local dev** environment
- Easy to undo (git)
- Low blast radius
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### ❌ Dangerous when...
- **Hosted / production** system
- Real users, real data
- Hard to undo
:::
::::

:::

::: notes
"YOLO mode" is the colloquial term — Claude Code calls it "auto-accept."
The key distinction: local dev agents operate in a sandbox where the worst case
is a git reset. Hosted prod agents can delete databases, email customers, or
corrupt data. Same technology, completely different risk profile. This
distinction — local dev vs. hosted prod — is a recurring theme today.
:::

## Hallucinations & sycophancy {.center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Hallucination

The model **invents** plausible-sounding information that isn't true.

_"The `pandas.DataFrame.fuzzy_merge()` method..."_

(This doesn't exist.)

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Sycophancy

The model **agrees** with you even when you're wrong.

_"You're right, that approach is much better!"_

(It wasn't.)

:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
Both are **unsolved**. Both are why we need human-in-the-loop design.
:::

::: notes
These aren't edge cases — they're fundamental properties of how LLMs work.
Hallucination comes from next-token prediction having no ground truth anchor.
Sycophancy comes from RLHF optimizing for human approval. Neither is going
away soon. The practical consequence: never trust LLM output that you can't
verify.
:::

---

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1.5em;"}

### I feel deeply conflicted about AI {style="color: #0e3b5c;"}

:::

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

I'm **so** much more productive.

But at what cost?

::: {.fragment}
**Personal** — Am I losing the ability to think for myself?
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Societal** — Am I contributing to something harmful?
:::

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.placeholder style="min-height: 300px;"}
IMAGE: something contemplative —<br>
a person at a crossroads, or a balance/scale visual<br>
(keep it tasteful, not melodramatic)
:::
:::
:::

::: notes
This is the vulnerable moment. Be genuine. The audience is probably feeling
some version of this too, especially at a conference like SciPy where
thoughtfulness about technology is valued. Don't resolve the tension — just
name it honestly. The point is to show that using these tools doesn't require
ignoring the hard questions.
:::

## What's the alternative? {.center}

::: {style="font-size: 1.2em; margin-bottom: 0.8em;"}
If I practice AI abstinence, is that better than<br>helping people practice **safe AI**?
:::

::: {.fragment}
I chose to build tools that make AI **safer and more transparent**:

- Humans stay in the loop
- Outputs are **verifiable**
- The system is **auditable**
:::

::: notes
The sex-ed analogy is deliberately provocative but apt. The alternative to
engagement isn't purity — it's ceding the space to people who won't build
guardrails. You've chosen to build tools (chatlas, querychat) that keep humans
in the driver's seat. That's the "safe AI" framing for the rest of the day.
:::

## In data science, correctness matters {.center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}

Design systems where humans can **course-correct** and **verify**.

- Show your work (code, not just answers)
- Make it reproducible
- Let the human say "no, that's wrong"

:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.placeholder style="min-height: 280px;"}
SCREENSHOT: Posit Assistant showing<br>
generated code alongside results —<br>
the "show your work" pattern
:::
:::
:::

::: notes
This is where your DS background pays off. In stats, we learned that showing
your methodology matters as much as showing your result. The same principle
applies to AI-assisted analysis: an app that shows the generated code lets the
user verify, reproduce, and correct. This is exactly what querychat does — but
we'll get there.

Mention your colleague's talk on correctness/reproducibility if it's at SciPy.
TODO(Carson): add the colleague's name and talk title.
:::

## Don't overthink it {.center}

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em; margin-bottom: 0.5em;"}
You probably don't need:
:::

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
### ~~Complex RAG~~
:::
::: {.column width="34%"}
### ~~Multi-agent systems~~
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
### ~~Fine-tuned models~~
:::
:::

::: {.fragment style="margin-top: 1em;"}
What you **do** need:

**Good context** + **good tools** + **a human in the loop**

Unless you have scale issues, agentic search of plain text files works surprisingly well.
:::

::: notes
This is the "permission to start simple" slide, and it's the bridge to the
rest of the tutorial. The three things we'll build with today — chatlas,
shinychat, querychat — are all context + tools + human-in-the-loop. No vector
databases, no fine-tuning, no multi-agent orchestration. You can go far with
the basics done well.
:::

## The recipe for today {.center}

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em; line-height: 1.8;"}

1. Focus LLMs on **language → code**
2. Give them **tools** and **context**
3. Keep **humans in the loop**

:::

::: {.fragment style="margin-top: 0.5em;"}
That's it. Let's go build.
:::

::: notes
This is the thesis slide — the three principles that underpin everything we
do for the rest of the day. Refer back to it at the start of each section.
Quick transition to break.
:::

# ☕ Break — 5 min {.center background-color="#0e3b5c"}

::: notes
Remind them: next up is chatlas — we're writing code. Make sure their API keys
/ LM Studio is working before we start.
:::
